overacting is a real style used by directors and actors but is often dismissed for bad acting because of the culture of american film goers when in reality it is typically used for humorous effect or to create a surreal atmosphere hey guys id like to take a minute to talk about over acting and how it is used basically since american cinema has no name for it ill just refer to it as over acting however japanese cinema does have a name for it i believe it is part of kabuki acting which is the traditional style of theatre acting i cant think of the actual name for it please inform me if you know it you can see examples of this in their cinema in movies like ran such as when the exiled emperor is going crazy american cinema also uses overacting but because it is less understood as being a part of the style of the film and has no traditional stance it is often miss interpreted as bad acting and then people cast off a movie for the wrong reasons what is overacting overacting is basically when an actor uses more emphasis or animation than what a typical person would use or how an audience would expect a person to react especially when the unimaginable is occurring why it is used overacting is used for a variety of reasons and often depends on the genre of the film in horror and comedy it is most often used for humorous or satirical reasons for instance the scream movies the killer ghost face is always over animated and uses cheesy lines to poke at traditional horror films where the killer always seems to be able to easily kill the side characters but screw up when trying to kill the main character it is regularly used in slapstick style comedy obviously for humorous purposes the three stooges is probably the best known example of this the collision of these two genres are great examples as well for instance evil dead or cabin in the woods both have a tongue in cheek humor that effectively uses overacting overacting in dramas is used to create a surreal feeling and that what your experiencing is more akin to something of a dream rather than something that is realistic or could actually occur perhaps the best director to use overacting effectively is james cameron other notable directors would be david lynch and stanley kubrick who have used over acting but not nearly as much in their films james cameron uses it to create a fairy tale effect kubrick and lynch used it as a way to dull the violence and drama of their films and make them more realistic by creating a dream like effect overacting is essential to camerons style it allows him to turn scenarios of a heartbroken drama into a fairy tale love story people often have said that a cameron film is more of an experience than a movie i would agree while overacting is essential to his films they would break down if they didnt have all the other elements to hypnotize them elements such as fantastic actors epic plots stunning visuals and well developed score but without overacting his movies would have more of a serious feeling to them than the fairy tale feeling to better understand this i would recommend reading the scripts to aliens and titanic in aliens really concentrate on bill paxtons character just notice how ridiculously cheesy all the lines look on paper but how when your watching the movie you seem to let that part slide by you cameron is basically using overacting as an element to his style which i would describe as semisurrealist camerons use of overacting does however differ from other directors such as david lynch and kubrick who use overacting simply for its surrealist effects to make things seem more dream like the tones of their films are much more serious violent and dramatic for instance the staircase scene in the shining jack nicholson is overacting as is shelly duvall as he explains to her how he is going to kill her and she poorly swings a bat at him lynch used it in blue velvet to take his incredibly strange film about evil believable by using extremely good actors to engage in these very weird scenes overacting can be used in other ways as well another very well known and notable example would be kevin spacey in the usual suspect spoiler alert do not read further if you plan on seeing the usual suspect if you watch when he is explaining his story within the movie during the present time he cant seem to deliver a line worth a damn but during his flashbacks and explanations he is suddenly a normal actor again thats because as you find out at the end the whole story is a lie that he is fabricating as he is going suddenly upon watching the movie again you realize hes lying the whole time he even sounds like hes lying i encourage you guys to go out and watch some of these films for yourself and make up your own opinion these are simply my thoughts about how overacting is used in american cinema just because an actor seems to be delivering something poorly stop for a second to look at the film as a whole and see if it fits the style you may find yourself having realizations about films and valuing them a lot more than you once did and if you learned thanks for reading and sorry and grammatic errors i ahh america in the 1950s the eisenhower era straightlaced and upstanding a peaceful society where the common man had a classy car a loving wife 2 12 children and a white picket fence to enclose his nuclear family in the atomic age wholesome wasnt it well not really slightly less innocent products of the 50s have survived to the present day than reruns of i love lucy if youve ever found yourself staring at a national enquirer issue selling an interview with the grassy knoll shooter or your tv blaring the newest sex scandal tmz uncovered you have the 50s to thank for that more specifically a 25 magazine called confidential while gossip tabloids have existed since at least 1916 confidential went much further in dredging up dirt for the masses than ever before creating a network of informants in hollywood and employing the latest spy gadgets in short setting a trend that others have since followed in earnest inspired by the popularity of the kefauver hearings confidential went after a target less capable of violent retaliation and from 19511957 confidential published exposés on hollywood figures some true some fake always salacious but the mafia did exist in hollywood perhaps from the very beginning more than a few studio heads are suspected of ties to organized crime and at least one of them columbia is known to have been founded with mob funds exactly how much power the mafia wielded in hollywood isnt known brute intimidation did not always succeed in securing casting slots or business deals mafia figures certainly enjoyed themselves in hollywood however establishing connections with movie stars and politicians and generally running amok with starlets one of these was johnny stompanato a violent mob enforcer primarily known today for being found dead in lana turners home in april 1958 alas confidential wasnt able to break one of the biggest scandals in hollywood history due to behindthescenes legal and political pressure from hollywood and the california attorney general confidential was forced to cease operations in 1957 and one of the editors would be found dead with his wife in the back of a nyc taxi cab months later in an apparent murdersuicide ___ presentation la confidential d by curtis hanson written by james ellroy brian helgeland kevin spacey russell crowe guy pearce 1997 imdb as corruption grows in 1950s la three policemen the straightlaced the brutal and the sleazy investigate a series of murders with their own brand of justice ___ in an awards season otherwise dominated by titanic la confidential became only the 3rd film to sweep the big four critics awards we remember the titanic for the crash the film is a celebration of tragedy same with the story of our lovers the story wouldnt be worth telling if jack lived on he has to die for their love to live on where is the tension of a film where we know what is going to happen we must invest drive into the ships sailing itself it is the sailing of a new love the iceberg is more than an iceberg it is a psychological signifier the iceberg is the unforgiving back hand of the father that separates the two lovers it represents the chaos of the real the over powering force of the super ego and our overdetermination by our past which limits our freedom their love like the ship is to be remembered for its destruction and early end it is made complete by its destruction on a boat there are no boundaries you can sail to each country of the world as the rich and poor mingle where there is no law in this empty vacuum of expectations our deeper selves come out our inner desires which society limits emerge like when you skinny dip and fingerbang in the woods in summer camp it allows for fantasies to emerge and be played out it is an open end of possibility a freedom which our two main characters yearn for each bound by their class the story of the travel to the new world is also one of attempted travel to a new identity unfortunately for our lovers the world is not smooth sailing their fantasy is just that a fantasy the over powering and uncontrollable reality of the iceberg crushes that fantasy it is the real they tried to escape re emerged as catastrophe the catastrophe is simply that which they avoided to get together bursting through like when you get away with something so long yourown feelings of guilt implicate you like when you cover your trail too well form the cookie jar it makes it obvious who stole cookies its the same logic here the drivers of the loveship thought they could power through reality with thier super boat but they outsmarted themselves and they were sunken by it all you cannot have a new identity you are pathetically you no matter how you try and escape it she learned nothing form him he gives her the wood and she takes it we can never plan when reality will pop up and interrupt and to an extent we cannot control our predetermination of how we deal with reality this would be the story of our lovers destruction had the ship sailed smoothly imagine her hoity self living in an apartment with him in a new york or boston ghetto for a few months i doubt the love would survive like all of these love stories they do everything in their power to work together but it is their spontaneous opposite reactions to emergences of the chaotic real that will drive them apart he can forgive and be self sacrificial while she is materialist and self involved you can almost picture them splitting up over her not being able to handle the living conditions food cleaning toilet petty things that have nothing to do with love or fantasy her demanding it all be clean up to snuff and him not wanting to keep up its how we deal with things like laundry cleaning and money real things that push us apart love and life are not cruise ships where these things are all forgotten she just resents her place in life and views the poor as more intimately connected with the spirit of it all he is the vanishing mediator that allows rose to continue her upper class life without guilt its like vampires the rich stealing the vigor of life from the poor etc he is a fantasy point through which rose can recast her identity within the uper class without actually leaving it she uses jack to rejuvenate herself by altering her relationship to her class by being at opposition from within a common trope for women in film stuck without any choice in life except love chooses love that recasts whole life tries to get freedom through her love usually ends up having to sacrifice the love to keep life but through the sacrifice the love is made real it is because they cant be together that they know it is real this is why every romantic film has a break up and a reconciliation it is always on the second try where we see the flaw and the person and want both that love is made we start viewing the other as a fantasy object then the image is broken we learn to accept them for their brutally real self and in some way that serves as a replacement and reaffirmation of the fantasy object to be destroyed by love is the most honest expression of it she learns that loves destroys the poor like everyone else so she is happy with her upper class love that feels so destructive i believe the guilt is more of a shame her excessive and uncontrolled sexual drive led her to have a man sacrificed and herself nearly killed and for what so she could live out her fantasy for a few days look at the film she does not want jack to stay as a real person only as a memory a point through which she an recast her upper class identity as somehow and forgive herself for not condemning it she pushes him into the water as saying she will never forget him ive seen terminator and terminator 2 both of which were pretty good but i didnt like aliens at all and titanic and avatar are probably my least favorite movies im not saying cameron is bad per se but i cant conceive why he is such a popular and critically acclaimed director i was just wondering if we could discuss american beauty in detail i myself have seem the movie twice and really enjoyed it upon second viewing but here are some questions i have 1 why in a time of largebudget stereotypical boxoffice hits like titanic and the matrix was this movie so commercially successful it seems like it would be a difficult film to convince someone to see being a characterdriven story 2 what did the water mean 3 what does colonel fitts represent in american society to call it one of the most overrated films in motion picture history is actually a doubtful statement it has been called the movie everybody loves to hate and if we stop for a moment and make a brief draft about films of the genre that are worse than titanic youll find out that the list is endless cameron feels unusual but not precisely unsuccessful remarkable score and effective melodrama overhyped is the correct adjective since it is far from being the best filmic adaptation of the story and not the first to add moving romance to the formula nonetheless it is a surprising effort76100 when it first set sail to the theaters in 1997titanicwas a boxoffice giant that broke all prior records and earned an unfathomable 600 million dollars in the us alone for 12 consecutive years it would sit upon this throne until finally surpassed in 2009 by james camerons other mega hit the epic science fiction wonderavatarbesides the astounding success in the financial department its also a technical marvel with awardwinning special effects that still look fantastic bymore the goodstarmaking turns from dicaprio and winslet intricate set design pretty good special effects the final third of the film the sinking scene ill never let go jack i promise and that last scene fuck certified tearjerker oh and naked kate winsletthe badoverlong oversentimental overloaded with cheesethe bottom linegod damn it im supposed to hate this film but for some reason it gets me every time i originally posted this in star wars but one of the commenters suggested it would be a good fit here back in the 1970s and 80s when george lucas was making his first star wars film he changed the way films would be made not just at a marketing and distribution level but on a deeply technical level he was ahead of time but in such a way that it was recognizable that this was the direction audiences wanted the world to go it would take a generation amid the democratization of both techniques and talent as industrial light magic employees left to join or found other shops before those techniques became more than just lucas and friends personal playthings but they did and when they did they became the way major blockbusters were created to the point where a dozen or so similar giant effects productions could be expected every summer then in the mid90s lucas announced the coming of the star wars prequel trilogy and his intention to do the same thing all over again he would not just create a new set of adventure films but a new paradigm for big budget technical filmmaking one where there were no bounds on a directors vision the camera the sets even the characters themselves would be unmoored from physical limitations they could be anything the filmmakers envisioned them to be do anything they were envisioned to do it was going to be a revolution on par with the release of the first star wars films and much marketing muscle was put into the effort emphasizing the rampant use of digital compositing and computergenerated world building and deemphasizing the more traditional tools used in the prequels production like models and full sets the result was probably not quite what lucas had in mind at least at first in the fifteen years since star wars episode i the phantom menace premiered the conventional wisdom on its and the other prequels failings has solidified at every level conceptual artistic technical it can be and is often recited with the drone of a prep school student conjugating ancient greek the casting particularly of anakin was bad the acting was wooden the dialog stilted it was frequently juvenile and the effects use had gone into overload eliminating elements of reality needed to suspend disbelief or more succinctly it didnt look like the old films it proved the conventional wisdom said that there was a limit to how much effects work should be turned over to the computer that a fantasy film needed to contain elements of the real to be believable locations puppets actors in costume and prosthetics full sets a minimum of blue screen that too much reliance on cgi had filmmakers throwing technology at problems rather than trying to think their way out of corners makers of competing franchises were quick to jump on that bandwagon once it was clear that audiences werent with the new thing making sure to put a spotlight on the traditional elements of their effects work and how they were doing things the old way the matrix was focused on story using its effects sparingly and as practical as possible the lord of the rings made sure to build its fantasy worlds through old school model photography as much as digital mattes and only made use of hordes of digital extras when they simply ran out of prosthetics and fake armor etc in all their myriad ways they told scifi and fantasy audiences were not the prequels and were not going to make the mistakes of the prequels it has become so engrained in conventional wisdom that when the star wars sequel trilogy was announced the producers made sure to note that they would be shooting on film not high definition video the next step of lucas digital focus and returning to more location photography and model work to try and recapture the aesthetic of the original films the mandate was clear the way of the prequels was dead overt cgi lack of traditional physical effects over reliance of digital compositing to create scenes and sequences these were enemies of the suspension of disbelief not creators of it they were the reasons at least the technical ones the prequels didnt work and the example it set for why no one should make films that way and yet now ten years later everyone makes films the prequel way after much hoopla surrounding the process and stylism of the first matrix the wachowskis and their cohorts went the full lucas to create legions of digital stunt doubles in order to create superhuman destruction and wirefu without the wires to facilitate the action moving from the presentday of the matrix to the realworld of the sequels location photography was replaced by major digital sets as computer generated powered armor combatted computer generated robots nor was that a one off transformation by one set of filmmakers when peter jackson returned to middleearth for his hobbit trilogy he threw out many of the classical techniques used on the lord of the rings films and embraced prequel style filmmaking gone were the 35mm arriflexs the actors in prosthetics the bigatures in their place high resolution red hd cameras digital make up and motion capture for creatures which had been fully prosthetic before and 100 use of digital matte paintings with far less location photography and more of the stage work done as green screen shrouded set extensions as opposed to fully realized set pieces the aging ian holm and christopher lee did not even need to travel to new zealand to film their scenes performing alone against blue screen instead and inserted into their sets and sequences after the fact much of the rationale as stated by jackson in his various commentaries around the new films is word for word the rationale behind similar decisions being made about the prequel trilogy digital photography has firmly taken hold not just in the still photography world where the change to digital eventually drove kodak into bankruptcy but in cinema as well as camera manufacturers rushed to make digital cinema cameras better and better amid increasing pressure from filmmakers by 2006 half of the blockbusters for that year such as superman returns and miami vice were shot digitally by 2010 that number had grown to 80 with films as diverse as the social network captain phillips skyfall and the avengers being captured digitally on alexas vipers and reds things had changed quite a bit from the years only recently past lucas shot his films in sensors were larger fiber optic bandwidth was faster tapes were out and dedicated solidstate hard drives recording uncompressed video were in the technological revolution lucas had led in and largely underwritten has been little noticed by current audiences as it has slowly but surely become the standard the same way lucas envisioned digital back lot has become an entrenched production technique with more and more productions guardians of the galaxy harry potter and the deathly hollows captain america the winter soldier man of steel the hunger games catching fire even the finale of the twilight saga breaking dawn having copied jacksons move and turned away from full sets and practical effects and towards large green screen wraps and digitally created sets all that exists of the planet xandar a walkway and some rooms the command ship of ronan a floor then filled to the brim with complicated computer generated characters like rocket raccoon listen to avengers director joss whedon talk about the production of the film and the two areas which cause him open anguish involve live locations helicopters over a desert and fully functional sets which dont work lokis prison there is even a small subset of filmmakers like zack snyder and robert rodriguez who have taken the lucas digital back lot and turned it up to eleven eschewing sets entirely in order to create entirely stylized worlds which actors will never set foot in in fact the use of prequel techniques has expanded far beyond what lucas initially attempted though he did predict it to not just set and creature creation but to every level of the classic production process including digitally created costumes used in green lantern the lantern corps and man of steel general zod and his followers and of course there is the giant standing over all of them james camerons avatar which went beyond the edge to throw out live action actors for large chunks of the film replacing them with digital counterparts living on entirely digital stages stuffed to the gills with digital flora and fauna recorded with cameras which not only didnt use film but didnt actually record images just tracking information for digital artists to fill in later on viewing the results these techniques were held up not as an obstacle to be overcome but the pillar on which the films success was based avatars two sequels in preproduction now for release late in the decade will likely push the envelope even further nor have we reached saturation when the prequels came out they boasted 2000 effects shots per film effectively every shot of each film 25 times as many as even the largest film had attempted at that point for context return of the jedi boasted about 400 effects shots titanic and fellowship of the ring had about 800 apiece it was a gauntlet thrown down announcing exactly how inculcated in the process digital technology could get last week victoria alonso marvels executive in charge of postproduction announced that avengers age of ultron would have 3000 effects shots produced by lucas industrial light and magic more than any film yet made the previous record holder will have been another marvel production guardians of the galaxy at 2750 even the relatively earth bound captain america the winter soldier managed a staggering 2500 in its two hour and sixteen minute running time none of which suggests feelings about even this side effect of the prequels are likely to change even as youngsters born during its creation come of age in its shadow conventional wisdom once set is hard to change in part because it is repeated so often and usually without consideration or thought and yet on this point at least lucas seems to have been right he did change the way things were done even if audiences were more ambivalent about it no matter what message audiences seem to take from the star wars prequels and their proscribed failings on a technical level at least filmmakers arent listening theyre repeating for most people indian film industry and bollywood are synonymous they represent movies produced by the indian film industry movies that have their own aroma of music dance drama and stories as someone who has grown up in india i too have seldom used or heard the term hindi film industry to a lot of us in india the term hollywood is synonymous with movies full of action and sexual tension movies with special effects and movies that show american popular culture quite loudly hollywood represents movies like titanic james bond the towering inferno jurassic park the arnold movies from total recall to terminator etc not to mention that we seldom differentiate between american and british cinema its all hollywood however as i have grown in my taste of cinema i have started to watch a wider variety of english films from oscar winning drama to old bw movies which are actually quite relevant to todays developing india movies that have no special effects or sexual tension that are plain conversations and have no technological twists and turns movies that talk about local american culture movies about americas cities and towns its nuances and details long point short i would just like to point out that there is much excellence and flavor in indian films beyond but not exclusive dance and music there is much beyond the bollywood tag so as a conversation i would love it if you could comment about indian films that you think go beyond the bollywood tag especially movies that are more recent movies created by the current generation of film makers from whom we can expect more edit bombay talkies is worth a mention here its relevant to the post bombay talkies is a 2013 indian anthology film consisting of four short films directed by anurag kashyap dibakar banerjee zoya akhtar and karan johar3 the film released on 3 may 2013 coinciding with and celebrating the centenary year of indian cinema and the beginning of a new era in modern cinema4 first here tired of the discussion first of allyes there is a huge chunk of this post that is copy and pasted from another comment i was recommending some taiwanese movies but i figured out that this huge wall of text is also a good way to introduce people to taiwanese filmmaking aside from the filmography of ang lee so without further adieu alright im taiwanese while i watching taiwanese movies the unfortunate thing about taiwanese cinema is that most of the time theaters show more foreign films than they do taiwanese movies its understandable why though taiwanese movies generally lack the production value or the filmmaking finesse that foreign movies show take this for example the most expensive movie ever made in taiwanese history cost about 2325 million us dollars to makeand because taiwanese cinema is still relatively young in development we dont have a lot of directors developed in their craft lets put it this way there are only a handful of taiwanese directors who are household names so unless ang lee makes another movie most taiwanese people tend to watch large budget foreign movies like the avengers when they head off to the theater but occasionally a really good nativetaiwanese movies are made with a wing and a prayer but because taiwanese movie production companies lack the financial capability of marketing these movies overseas they only tend to stay around asia thus creating some really geocentric masterpieces that i think people in the west will never get to see because theyre not advertised that said here are a few recommendations im going to recommend 3 movies for now 3 taiwanese movies that are all different in genre the only thing in common about them are that theyre really well known in taiwan and around east asia and that theyve all mcmurphy triumphs over nurse ratched other times we feel frustration at the stifling institutional control or even seethe with absolute anger and ultimately we weep at the tragedy inherent with the tethering of an individualist soul to an uncomprehending authority the narrative of the film follows randal p mcmurphy jack nicholson a convict who has been sent to a mental health institution for evaluation it is made clear later in the film that mcmurphy is essentially faking any mental issues in an effort to serve the remainder of his sentence in the relatively cushy psyche ward of course he had no idea that the sinister nurse ratched louise fletcher would be waiting for him far more dangerous than any prison warden the crux of the story and conflict involves the interaction between these two titanic forces mcmurphy the freethinking individualist and ratched the domineering sometimes ratched wins the tilt and sometimes mcmurphy does though the result can turn on a single moment one of the more important battle of wills occurs over mcmurphys desire to watch the world series at group one day he suggests that the schedule be changed so that he and the other men on the ward can watch the baseball game ratched puts it to a vote and few of the men are on mcmurphys side later that evening in the game room mcmurphy proves to everyone else just how intent he is on watching that game he tries to lift a gigantic plumbing fixture from its foundations and throw it through the window though ultimately fails mcmurphys desire for freedom impresses all of the men who witness this feat so when charlie brings up that a second game of the world series deserves a second vote it is a landslide all nine members of the therapy group raise their hands ratched calmly trumps them by pointing out that it would take a majority vote and there are a total of 18 members of the psyche ward mcmurphy cant believe she is counting the mentally challenged members but realizes he only needs one additional vote he scours the room for one more raised hand and ratched closes the meeting ultimately the deafmute indian affectionately named chief seems to understand and raises his hand but ratched will not be beaten the vote has been closed and there will be no baseball game until mcmurphy sits down in front of the tv and nicholson puts a twinkle in his eye to indicate the germination of a brilliant idea mcmurphy stares at the tv and begins announcing a baseball game the men wander out from their room and mcmurphy continues they look up at the screen dumbfounded but assume that since mcmurphy sees it it must be happening as the radio play continues ratched looks on enraged that she has been beaten and worried that her control of the men on the ward is slipping one flew over the cuckoos nest dramatizes this struggle between mcmurphy and ratched for the hearts and minds of the other men on the ward mcmurphy treats the men as sovereign individuals capable of taking control of their own lives and living well whereas ratched is content to coddle them control them and constrict their movements and freedom for their own sake of course but isolated moments hint at a darker motivation beneath her surface ultimately her insidious nature is revealed in the climax of the film during a goingaway party for mcmurphy who is about to escape the young billy sleeps with one of the girls mcmurphy invited the next morning billy is discovered by ratched and he actually manages to stand up to her for once until she skewers him with the one thing she knows will hurt him most i cant help but think of what your mother will say billy immediate retreats and begs nurse ratched to not tell his mother but ratched knows how important billys mother is in his life and she insists that she experiences a sadistic glee in doing so billy suicides is inevitable and when ratched tries to skirt the blame mcmurphy nearly throttles her to death tragically this results in a treatment for mcmurphys dangerous unhealthy aggression a lobotomy when he is returned to the ward the chief revealed to be faking his deafmute ailment goes to tell mcmurphy that he is finally ready to take the support of the group and actually manages to throw it out the window and escape this wakes the men who believe at least for the moment that mcmurphy has succeeded and escaped the film ends on the leonardo dicaprios career may have just reached its apex with his remarkable performance in the revenant 2015 and subsequent oscar win finally however his career includes a number of other impressive performances ranging from playing a poor but charming young artist on rms titanic to a corporate spy who infiltrates the very dreams of his victims on this starstudded resume there are two films that stand out because of the remarkable similarity between the characters that leo plays in them in both the wolf of wall street 2013 and the great gatsby 2013 he dons the guise of a suave goodlooking millionaire who cheats his way to fortune from lowly beginnings only to experience a precipitous fall from grace at the climax of the movie one could even call the wolf of wall street somewhat of a spiritual successor to the great gatsby both stories are about the failings of the american dream portrayed through the meteoric rise and abrupt downfall of the main character heck the movies even came out only a little under 8 months apart from one another but where the movies differ substantially is in their treatment of excess in both movies this takes a primary role in the story but the wolf of wall street does an almost incomparably better job of portraying this extravagance in this essay i will explore the ways in which baz luhrmann missed the mark in his depiction of excess in the great gatsby i absolutely love the story of gatsby and f scott fitzgeralds original novel is one of my alltime favorites i think fitzgeralds depiction of the failures of the american dream is one of the most hardhitting and deeply relevant lessons we can learn in contemporary america and his decision to portray this moral though the failures of gatsby is absolutely brilliant both at a dramatic and rhetorical level because of how much i adore the story i was incredibly nervous when i heard that baz luhrmann was going to be directing the movie adaptation this is the guy who made a modern adaptation of romeo and juliet that used the original shakespearean dialogue but couched it in a contemporary setting that featured car crashes pool sex and guns that means that characters go around yelling put your swords up and people pull out their guns you cant make this stuff up i can appreciate trying to adapt a movie to more modern times but when you turn the epic duel between romeo and tybalt into this frankly confusing mess of a scene i think you have to reconsider how effective your directing strategies are the whole movie is just unsettling its so strange to watch characters act out a decidedly shakespearean era story using shakespeares own dialogue in a modern setting with modern tools and tropes it really just doesnt work the morals of shakespeares play dont translate over because luhrmann makes no attempt whatsoever to actually adapt the themes of romeo and juliet to contemporary society instead making solely aesthetic or otherwise superficial changes hes trying to fit a square peg into a round hole but despite my misgivings i was still pretty excited for the movie i liked the cast and i was hoping that luhrmann would have learned from his mistakes with romeo and juliet boy was i wrong as much i loved the book this movie was honestly just painful to watch it wasnt all bad leo delivered a terrific acting performance and the core of the story was still there albeit diminished but it was just so excruciating to watch the great gatsby fall prey to almost the exact same issues that had plagued romeo and juliet just like before luhrmann took a story that is defined by its setting 1920s america and needlessly embellished it with a distinctly contemporary aesthetic elements as one critic put it luhrmann is exactly the wrong person to adapt such a delicately rendered story and his 3d feature plays like a ghastly roaring 20s blowout at a sorority house he robs fitzgeralds wellcrafted writing of its trademark intricacy and replaces it with a poorly conceived façade of meaningless extravagance but dont just take my word for it here is a video showcasing the first party in the great gatsby i would recommend watching it before continuing on with this essay here is a corresponding section from fitzgeralds novel that describes the same party there was dancing now on the canvas in the garden old men pushing young girls backward in eternal graceless circles superior couples holding each other tortuously fashionably and keeping in the corners and a great number of single girls dancing individualistically or relieving the orchestra for a moment of the burden of the banjo or the traps by midnight the hilarity had increased a celebrated tenor had sung in italian and a notorious contralto had sung in jazz and between the numbers people were doing stunts all over the garden while happy vacuous bursts of laughter rose toward the summer sky a pair of stage twins who turned out to be the girls in yellow did a baby act in costume and champagne was served in glasses bigger than fingerbowls the moon had risen higher and floating in the sound was a triangle of silver scales trembling a little to the stiff tinny drip of the banjoes on the lawn the movies depiction of the party is a hot mess compared to fitzgeralds colorful writing there is no intricacy in luhrmanns scene no complex layers representing the multitude of different experiences occurring under one roof no room for anything but a single kind of display of excess every single thing on the screen is just so visually striking that at the end of the day nothing pops out to the eye it all just blurs together when i watch the scene from 247 to 316 of the video im struck by how little i recall of it afterwards really all i remember is that there were a bunch of people dancing and then some streamers came down is that really what fitzgeralds magnum opus has been reduced to fitzgeralds description of the scene is so superior that even though there are no later but 1913 was the time for three major films from three different countries to shine well alright i included frances fantômas on a technicality its actually a serial that didnt have all of its episodes released until 1914 but its a good place to start considering its one of the most entertaining and quickmoving silents that doesnt rely off a specific movement to keep its pace going soviet montage for example it simply blazed by for its own merits and it worked incredibly the director of fantômas louis feuillade would have other masterworks through the 1910s such as les vampires judex drastically in later years and unlike feuillade i will certainly be covering his other films here ingeborg holm was just the boost he needed to grow finally the last masterwork of 1913 and my favorite of the three denmark proved that the 1910s were its peak years in cinema beginning with august bloms most famous feature atlantis august blom remains one of the strongest filmmakers of the entire silent era a true auteur who risen above his peers in an already strong danish film market not as initially referenced as later work in the silent era such as birth of a nation but that was due to the sudden shift in impact for feature films gone was a lot of the european influence and onwards came the cultural capital of itself the united states of america it largely stayed in the dark throughout 1913 but exploded onto the scene in 1914 1914 was americas year in the theater with three huge debuts into the feature film landscape the first being the 1910s darling that was maurice tourneur with romance and while the perils of pauline was another important early serial one of the directors would retire a few years later the other would go on to make reefer madness elsewhere benjamin christensen got a very big first impression with the mysterious x which further establish the early silent era as denmarks best showing in cinema history we also had the limp return of another old full article with images at sometime before 1972 brian de palma read an article in life magazine about conjoined twins from russia named masha and dasha krivoshlyapova immediately after birth the girls were whisked away by doctors and their mother was falsely informed that her daughters had perished the sisters lived most of their young lives confined in nightmarish captivity subjected to horrific torture by the scientific sector of the soviet regime they were joined at the hip and shared a reproductive system bladder and colon while possessing separate hearts lungs spines and small intestines although similar in physical appearance their personalities differed tremendously masha was a dominant borderline psychopath capable of extreme violence toward the timid more reserved dasha this bizarre duplicity brewed a diabolical idea in de palma that would later become sisters its no wonder the horrifying story of masha and dashas adolescence resonated with the legendary director after all much of his childhood was spent in operating rooms watching his father an orthopedic surgeon slice through the flesh of comatose strangers margot kidder plays a charming young model and aspiring actress named danielle and doubles as her churlish twin sister dominique during the taping of a television game show danielle meets david lisle wilson and the two of them hit it off after repeatedly dodging her alleged exhusband william findley they escape to her apartment and end the night with a steamy tryst on her pullout couch the next morning david is brutally stabbed to death from her apartment on high investigative journalist grace collier jennifer salt witnesses the horrible scene some bumbling police officers reluctantly respond to her frantic call already irritated due to a series of scathing columns previously written by grace when they finally get to danielles apartment theres no sign of any disturbanceno bloody corpse no murder weapon just the dainty russian model in her pink nightgown not one to accept defeat grace vows to separate the truth from this distorted report it becomes something of a crooked obsession leading her on a path filled with dangerous people and demented secrets de palma is a devoted pupil of alfred hitchcocks catalogue his thriller pays admirable homage to films like rope 1948 and rear window 1954 but overall the film is molded in the image of hitchcocks psycho 1960 as the layers of the story slowly peel away danielle and dominique remain an enigma to give away more may spoil the plot although its predictable enough to do so on its own still there is much to be found in sisters aside from the horror identity so clearly inspired by the master of suspense the editing is quick and merciless one moment then patient and careful in the next the camera glides over the characters watching their lives like an observant ghost like a curious member of the audience that stood up in the theater and crept into the screen de palmas eye is precise and inquisitive lingering on specific objects like a tiny blood stain or a colorful birthday cake if only to lure the audience closer into his odd world his use of split screen is also quite remarkable through the intense pairing of similarly charged images such as the frantic cleaning of a gore splattered apartment beside cops ambling in to investigate he succeeds in conjuring desperate tension and infectious palpable dread the impressively talented cast helps to alleviate the strain of the imitative plot by bravely disappearing into their complex characters kidder is strangely funny and unusually disturbing salt is triumphant as the heroine perfectly embodying a powerful woman with a passion for justice and discovery grace isnt some damsel in need of a savior but she eventually enlists the help of a wise cracking private investigator played by character actor charles durning as a villainous stalker william findleys mysterious performance is effectively unsettling and equally hilarious the mindless police officers that cant show up on time or find any useful slivers of evidence contribute to the dark humor de palma sews beneath the skin of sisters his titanic knack for cultivating vibrant characters sprouted early in his career and would bloom beautifully throughout his future films missteps in the film that escape the redeeming reach of the cast are thankfully lifted by an eerie score from hitchcocks legendary goto composer bernard herrmann his music is both gorgeous and grotesque never allowing the listener to remain with one sentiment for too long when hearing his work not only in sisters but in other classics like vertigo 1958 or during marion cranes awful death in the shower at bates motel we begin to understand why hitchcocks movies were so compelling his meticulous filmmaking techniques and wonderful casting choices undoubtedly provided a strong core for greatness but herrmanns instrumentals allowed them to transcend the ranks of ordinary cinema he was able to translate the emotional charge of the visuals then turn around and perfectly interpret his findings into dazzling musical arrangements he generously does this for de palmas film smoothing many rough edges along the way the shock and horror in sisters sprouts from hidden truths that never really rise to the surface within the reality of the film evils emerge and escape punishment by the thinnest of margins this speaks volumes to the respectable effort in de palmas attempt at creating his own version of psycho many elements strike with great rigor and poise yet they ultimately fall short weighed down by disproportionate design this isnt the first time that brian de palma would rip pages from the book of hitchcock although what he gave us here was good enough for a start it would get much better in the short years to come as he mastered the balance between narrative and technique style and story when sisters finally comes to an end were left high on a perch with durnings private eye as he patiently watches for something interesting to happen hoping for some sort of evidence to make logical sense of the jumbled commotion found in the interesting discussion and debate after what seems like a lifetime of delays cancellations and reschedules it follows director david robert mitchells newest film under the silver lake finally lands on vod and digital release this week the film is the story of 33 year old perpetually inebriated slacker sam as he scours the weird underbelly of los angeles in search of sarah the cute girlnextdoor who has suddenly gone missing the film is the latest to emerge in a genre i will aptly call stonoire a subgenre of noire that combines dense raymond chandleresque mystery stories with the offbeat humor and absurdism expected from the stoner genre over the years this subgenre of noire has produced films such as the coen brothers the big lebowski and paul thomas andersons inherent vice both being films that initially divided audiences only to be welcomed in open arms by the world of cult film fandom under the silver lake certainly continues that trend of initial divide as the film seems to be so polarizing that it divides audiences into two camps absolutely love it or absolutely loathe it so what is it about this type of story that leads to this kind of divide well to answer that question we must first trace the subgenre back to its hazy beginnings while elements of this kind of story can be traced back to the raymond chandler adaptations that helped kickstart the hollywood machine back in the 1930s the big lebowski was the flagship film that brought all of these elements together to form a coherent subgenre lebowski offers a unique blend of comedy and mystery that doubles as a detective thriller and a commentary on societal trends our protagonist the ever likeable dude is a zen slacker who lets the world wash over him like waves on a beach this blend of passive protagonist hardly feels at home in a detective story but the fish out of water elements at play here offer countless opportunities for slow burn comedy that have undoubtedly helped lebowskis longevity but another part of the lasting appeal of lebowski is the social commentary packed under the absurdity the dudes apathy towards things like wealth status and power throughout the story underlines the coens ever present themes of nihilism tying it all together in the cyclical metaphor of bowling its one of those rare films that benefits from repeat viewings but its absurd borderline esoteric sense of comedy keeps each viewing fresh and funny the films hidden density played a large factor in its poor box office performance but as the years went on and the high of lebowski started to settle in with the rest of the world it became a bona fide classic with both the world of cult filmgoers in the years that followed lebowski the film world and the world at large underwent a rapid change that ultimately resulted in a society fit ready for lebowski the post 911 world was becoming increasingly cynical weary to any attempt at hollywood made at instilling emotion and sentiment this coupled with the increased connectivity of an emerging digital world gave birth to a new blend of media consumer this new consumer more in tune to how the sausage is made sought complexity this new arena for media consumption took in lebowski with open arms and by the 2010s the film had become not only revered among its cult following but among more traditional film buffs in march of 2010 titan of film criticism roger ebert added lebowski to his great movies collection elevating his previous score of ¾ stars to the full 4 it had taken over a decade but the world had finally seen lebowski for what it truly was by the time the 2010s had swung in the blossoming cynicism of the post 911 world had started to sprout into full blown paranoia the interconnectedness of the digital age showed everyone just how horrible the world could be increasing acts of terrorism around the world and the ensuing rat race between competing news outlets to get their version of the story out there first started to form a distrust between the media and the people with the increasing ease of media manipulation a shadow of a doubt emerged in the public consciousness with every emerging news story filmmaker paul thomas anderson fresh off the critical successes of there will be blood and the master sought to tackle this emerging paranoia with his next film an adaptation of thomas pynchons 2009 novel inherent vice the story follows doc sportello a private detective living on the beaches of southern california during the dawn of the 1970s when manson induced paranoia was in full swing the film follows the books labyrinthian plot in which doc comes to the aid of his former girlfriend who needs help foiling a plot to send her new boyfriend a real estate mogul into the looney bin inherent vice in many ways acted as a spiritual successor to lebowski both feature lead characters smoking their way through a mystery that becomes increasingly dense with each revelation both feature colorful supporting characters that provide a lot of the comedy through their interactions with the protagonist both feature an america in transition lebowski with the transition from the reagan era into the 90s and vice with the transition from the flower power of the 1960s and into the post manson world where vice differs however is in its execution lebowski used its comedy to unearth the philosophy underneath and vice uses its philosophy to unearth the comedy inherent vice leans a lot heavier into its time period than lebowski does its hard to even remember lebowski as a period piece the film took place in 1991 while being released in 1998 so the periodness of it was lost to the audience at the time inherent vice from the getgo paints the post manson world as an era of rapid and sudden change housing developments are gentrifying the hippie stuffed beaches fear of satanic cults have made their way into the execution of law enforcement marijuana has fell by the wayside in favor of heroin the film is fascinated with the culture clash of the tenacious hippies trying to keep the 60s alive and the brute force of the 70s kicking in the door its these culture crashes that create the comedy one of the funniest scenes of the film comes from doc sportello watching in confused awe as detective bigfoot bjornsen kicks in sportellos door and eats all of his marijuana this style of comedy while leaning a little harder on absurd in comparison to lebowski results in a film that left moviegoers puzzled and the cult fandom enthralled while inherent vice was a commercial flop its place among film critics was immediately recognized critics praised the film as the next evolution in the emerging genre that lebowski had paved before and the film even managed to make it onto the bbcs critic poll of the 100 greatest films of the 21st century only two years after the films release with vice it was clear that the genre was capable of more and david robert mitchell fresh off the success of 2015s it follows decided to tackle the genre with his next film under the silver lake under the silver lake take elements from both lebowski and vice but still manages to create a film that feels completely different we once again follow a stoned detective as he stumbles through southern california looking for answers the film takes notes from lebowski and also offers a period piece of the not so distant past it takes place in los angeles in 2010 far enough back to recognize sport odd fashion choices but not far enough back that it feels like a different time the film also offers its fair share of colorful characters such as the woman in the balloon dress a topless woman with a parrot and a pirate under the silver lake however takes these elements and ups the absurdity to the point where it almost feels like a david lynch film from the beginning the film shows an obsession with the symbols and messages that make up our media our protagonist sam hopelessly hoards old media such as classic films and old issues of nintendo power looking for meaning he is at his wits ends he has been let go from a job we never learn about and has recently parted ways with a girlfriend we dont meet until over an hour into the film he is long overdue on rent and soon to be evicted but that doesnt stop him from pursuing his next door neighbor after she mysteriously disappears after meeting him sam cant find meaning in his own life so he seeks it in media under the silver lake manages to preserve the comedic essence of lebowski and vice while also amping up its absurdist elements to craft a story about the meaningless of media lebowski and vice had its fair share of characters that bordered on ridiculous from a cocaine addicted dentist with a penchant for young women to the landlord with a passion for interpretive dance but these characters while strange maintained a level of realism that made them nothing more than exaggerations of los angeles archetypes under the silver lake however features multiple characters that are so absurd and fantastical that you are left wondering whats real and whats just an acid flashback characters like the songwriter the owls kiss and the homeless king are so extreme in their design and what they represent that the film almost feels like a horror film at times unsurprising from the mind behind it follows these absurdist creations result in cryptic scenes meant to be analyzed and never truly understood resulting in a story that feels equal parts lebowski and vice with a little bit of mulholland drive thrown in for good measure its hard to say right away whether or not under the silver lake will have the same longevity of lebowski or vice the film undoubtedly has already become a new source of obsession for cult film enthusiasts a blossoming online scene of armchair analysts has already begun to emerge decoding each and every frame of the film looking for messages and meaning but its that density and abundance of visual metaphors that has proven to be just as infuriating for some as it has fascinating to others regardless of its longevity under the silver lake just seems like the logical next step to the stonoire doubling down on the meaninglessness and upping the absurdity to nearly lynch levels it almost makes you wonder where can it go from here all it really tells you is not comedy but even then some dramas can have comedic elements as well seven pounds to kill a mockingbird titanic forrest gump good will hunting the green mile lawrence of arabiawhat do these movies honestly have in common besides the label drama attached to them honestly not very much the best definition i can think of for drama films is that they all have a certain look and feel and usually center around interpersonal relationships between there are very few people on earth who havent seen at least one spielberg film and even fewer who say that his films arent fantastic and yet despite this hes rarely regarded as one of the greats alfred hitchcock stanley kubrick martin scorcesse akira kurosawa andrei tarkovsky ingmar bergman jean luc goddard francis ford coppola and even modern day directors like david lynch david fincher christopher nolan and bong joon ho are popular choices for the great film directors and yet the highest grossing film director of all time the one with arguably the greatest impact on cinema is rarely put on this list thinking it over theres a few arguments that pop up against the man and id like to discuss a few of them a great craftsman not a great artist a common one is that spielberg makes technically sound films but theyre not art i agree that in terms of the craft few directors compare his immaculate long takes with fluid blocking tight and exciting editing and his good ol pal john williams to swell the score at just the right moment all come together to make the most well watchable movies ever the same way the great writers makes you forget youre reading a book spielberg makes you forget youre watching a movie but his films arent art i strongly disagree jaws raiders of the lost ark et empire of the sun jurassic park saving private ryan and especially schindlers list are all emotionally resonant and thematically deep even genre films like jaws raiders and jurassic park are still worthy of discussion no i dont believe he made these films with the intent to make the audience think like with 2001 a space odyssey but the fact that we return to these films again and again decades after they released means that theres something there i saw et for the second time recently and i was more emotional this time than before even though i knew the ending and every plot beat that led to it if something is that rewatchable and emotionally resonates with you why isnt that worth studying and this goes even more to films like empire of the sun and schindlers list which are full of symbols and ambiguous meanings that are worth studying more on these kinds of films later hes had a bad impact on cinema and only seeks dollar signs like i mentioned in my intro his impact is lets face it the biggest of all filmmakers hes reinvented genres and the entire industry again and again and again invented the summer blockbuster with jaws ushered in the age of sci fi with close encounters and et revived the exotic adventure genre with raiders of the lost ark paved the way for cgi with jurassic park made the first mainstream holocaust film with schindlers list and set the template for all war movies with saving private ryan in style aesthetic and look but many argue that his impact is for the worse i wont go into every single era but the general argument is that his success has lead to the cheapening of cinema as studios chase enormous box office returns with even bigger budgets leaving less room for the more personal small scale films first of i dont think spielberg himself should be blamed for the trends he sets hes just out to make a movie by that logic we should blame nolan for popularizing the dark and gritty superhero or blame james cameron for the dozens of historical romance movies that came out in the wake of titanics success if you want to blame someone for the cheapening of cinema then someone like michael bay and the disney corporation are more liable targets for such accusations but again this all assumes that studios and people arent out to make money every film director ever has had one goal and thats to make a movie that will please people and by proxy make money spielberg is no different and for such a titanically successful director his films arent always commercial successes bfg came out as early as 2016 and was an enormous bomb munich would have suffered the same fate if not for overseas markets and amistad and empire of the sun barely made due with what they made theres a reason he doesnt take a salary when making films and thats because he puts his time and effort on the line if a movie fails then he fails with it but sure hes just chasing dollar signs he should stay in his lane ie stick to making blockbusters but ok most people can look past this and enjoy his blockbusters for what they are which is expertly crafted and emotionally riveting but then hell go ahead and make empire of the sun or schindlers list or saving private ryan or munich etc i think people would be more comfortable praising him if he stayed in his lane ie only made blockbusters not serious historical films you look at alfred hitchcock who as far as i know never made a movie based on a true story and today hes known as the master his movie vertigo recently displaced citizen kane as the so called greatest film ever in the sight and sound 2012 critics poll but no such honor is reserved for a single spielberg film in that very same poll its only in the directors poll that a spielberg film makes the list and only one that being jaws at 76 the placement of the movie isnt my issue its that there wasnt even a consideration for anything else its clear that much of the cinema elite dont take spielberg seriously and regard his serious films as just flaccid attempts to be taken seriously but i see this as a massive disrespect and distasteful look who are we to say what a director should and shouldnt do isnt the point of a movie to see what this person and the ones behind him have to say look at stanley kubrick or howard hawks whose films run the gambit in terms of genre plot and style but after 2001 people didnt say that kubrick should make only sci fi films or after rio bravo people didnt demand that hawks make westerns forever they went on to do different things challenge themselves and not repeat the same old thing and i believe spielberg is the same way yes his films have recurring themes and styles because of course they do every director does some more than others but just look at this past decade a world war 1 film about a horse a political drama about lincoln a courtroom dramaespionage thriller a whimsical adaptation of a classic fairy tale a true story journalism picture and a fantastical adventuregame movie that takes place mostly in a virtual word the man has 30 plus films to his name and yet his very next film will be a musical something hes never done before not all of these films are perfect or even good and im honestly wary of his remake of west side story but ill always appreciate his drive to challenge himself and not give a damn about staying in his lane is spielberg underrated in a strange way its these arguments that i think influence people however subconsciously to disregard spielbergs films as serious art again not all his films are masterpieces and even his great films have flaws but when he gets it right he gets it fucking right and yet hes not taken seriously in that sense spielberg is an underrated filmmaker but wait how can the director with the biggest impact the highest grossing film director of all time by a wide margin be underrated well because today hes seen as just another filmmaker his modern day films are liked but not considered classics which is a shame as he continues to push the boundaries and make classics whether its the technical and emotional masterpiece that is catch me if you can the dark complexity of munich or the steadfast spirit of bridge of spies he still makes movies worth remembering even his bad movies are still miles ahead of the average schlock that gets pushed through because theres still an identity and a soul to his pictures which makes his masterpieces like schindlers list all the more unbelievable still not one of the greats yet overall im disappointed that spielberg isnt seen as one of the greats like with so many of the ones that inspired him like with so many of his contemporaries or like with the dozens of modern day filmmakers that were inspired by him but i do take comfort in the fact that as time passes his stature will only grow with time as more and more people discover his films and do away with the silly preconceptions that many people have about his films to reference alfred hitchcock one last time he too was seen as nothing but a technical master not worthy of being called art but growing up there was one young kid with dreams of making his own films who held no shame in calling him the master that kid was steven spielberg and i believe that years from now there will be hundreds of critics and filmmakers who will give that moniker to spielberg one he rightfully deserves my header was originally going to be do you think the events of sandy hook could ever be made into a fictitiously reconstructed nondocumentary film despite it obviously being too long and heavyloaded for an opening thats the specific question that routed me to a subject thats been lingering with me for a while when does a representation of a historical tragedy in any fictionalized form and of any medium like film television literature etc sink into exploitation storytelling is a way for us to not only find catharsis with trauma and despair but to also help potentially reframe our thinking of the world and even ourselves andor build on to values we already hold maybe for the better or worse as broad as that is i think theres an extra set of baggage to the responsibility and morality that comes into evaluation with representing trauma from a historical tragedy in particular wars terrorist attacks mass shootings genocides and events like the sinking of the titanic have been shown in some way or another across history in varying narrative disciplines but it comes with a catch when gilbert gottfried joked about 911 a few weeks after it happened someone from the audience shouted what would become a standard label for this type of comedy too soon now it seems perfectly acceptable and predictable to some extent to joke about 911 i cant say i havent laughed at louis cks crack at it yet i feel like comedy in general gets some leverage on addressing socialhistorical tragedy as long as the joke doesnt get too morbid and dense however the too soonness of comedy applies to drama as well if paul greengrass wrote july 22 a year after the event could it have been made at that time my answer is easily no but the timing behind addressing anything of this nature through fictionalized reenactment depends on the event itself greengrasss united 93 dealt directly with 911 five years after it happened and oliver stones world trade center came out only a few months later even though these two examples are specifically based off of real people that are reenacted the sensitivity of representing events like these arent diminished when the film doesnt base its story off of specific places people and events for instance it still wouldve obviously been too soon for gus van sant to make elephant two or three years after columbine even though he didnt name names or strive to create a historical reenactment in general only in partial influence but the reference was clear enough the same indirect approach was mitigated in patriots day only three years after the boston bombing where a fictional cop is a guide to follow through all the actual events that took place while also functioning as an amalgamated essence of the frontline workers who served during it what this also got me thinking about is why this managed to get greenlit so shortly after this occurred when it took so much longer for us to start confronting something like columbine or 911 again excluding efforts like 25th hour which puts it in the background but still makes it essential for mood and character development  i think its simply just because the gravity of whatever event itself as in the level of devastation in a societal context weighs whats acceptable and what isnt for the time despite how its represented it wont matter if the story uses real people as characters or real settings there are some nerves that arent ready to be touched but i feel like some can never really be in narrative form films like the ones ive already mentioned can be debated for whether they sink into exploitation or even propaganda whether they touch on something headon united 93 wtc july 22 or dont but still hit all of the familiar metaphorical and reframed beats war of the worlds cloverfield elephant discounting whatever narrativenondocumentary approach is taken im still a bit skeptical that something like sandy hook can be put on film nonetheless if stories coming from within concentration camps can be adapted to the screen in all its horror and grittiness decades later and if someone can write a fictional love story set over the titanic after nearly a century of course most of the survivors were probably dead by the time but still it seems a little more plausible that maybe we could one day get a movie about sandy hook not to say that im asking for one maybe and hopefully it wont go too far into detail and graphically depict children being shot in school but that leads me to ask again if all tragedies should be approached with some degree of restriction to what they show when filmed no matter how truthful they need to be part of the question brings to light the moral responsibility of the filmmakers in how respectful delicate and sensitive they are to those who actually suffered yet you could say that placing reservations like these could get in the way of truthfully representing the essence and terror of what happened as brian coxs robert mckee said in adaptation wow them in the end and youve got a hit which certainly applies to et the 6 month mark communications with earth was lost and a crew member was killed in an accident through process of elimination we can easily deduce that it was the junior engineer who is killed who we see in the early scenes smiling and talking about the son he misses back home on earth really much later in the film we get to the moment where communications is knocked out by a solar flare and of course my brain which despite my best efforts i can never shut off while screening films kicks in and tells me well this is the scene where the junior engineer dies theres not a great deal of tension the scene even plays with a misdirection implying the senior engineer will die but we know he wont the method of death is tragic and dramatic and i think i would have liked it more if the movie itself didnt spoil this for me so what was the point of this was there a thematic reason i am not getting here the movie also telegraphs several other story beats by way of cutting repeatedly to interviews clearly set after the mission was complete does the story gain anything from this nonlinear fashion or is it as i suggested earlier there because it is designed for an audience that lives in that
